raw_response:
## Overall Assessment

**P1.1 is not ready.** Meridian HR has a public privacy policy, but the notice does not fully or accurately describe several material processing activities evidenced in the ROPA, consent records, and DSAR log. The most significant gaps relate to employee-facing notice in a B2B2C model, unclear controller/processor roles, omitted sensitive-data processing, inaccurate analytics descriptions, incomplete third-party disclosures, and retention inconsistencies.

---

## Findings

| # | Finding Title | Related Document / Section | Why It’s a Gap | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Employee data subjects may not receive effective notice | Privacy Policy §§1, 7, 9; SOC 2 readiness checklist P1.1; consent records | Meridian’s privacy policy is written as a public-facing policy, but most data subjects are employees of client organizations. The policy tells employees to contact their employer for privacy rights, and material changes are communicated only to customer administrators. There is no evidence that Meridian ensures employees actually receive Meridian’s privacy notice or that employer clients are contractually required to provide equivalent notice covering Meridian’s processing. For P1.1, notice must be provided to data subjects, not only to client administrators. | High |
| 2 | Controller vs. processor roles are unclear and potentially misleading | Privacy Policy §§1, 4, 7; ROPA PROC-001 through PROC-012; consent records | The policy does not clearly distinguish when Meridian acts as a processor for employer clients versus when Meridian acts as an independent controller. The ROPA shows Meridian acts as controller for website analytics, sales CRM, email marketing, customer support, and workforce benchmarking. However, the privacy policy generally presents employee platform processing as employer-directed and tells employees to contact their employer. This is incomplete where Meridian independently determines purposes, such as benchmarking analytics or direct marketing. | High |
| 3 | Wellness survey processing is not adequately disclosed | Privacy Policy §§2, 3, 4, 6, 7; ROPA PROC-005; consent records WC-0001 through WC-0012 | The ROPA shows Meridian processes voluntary wellness survey data, including stress levels, work-life balance, job satisfaction, and self-reported mental health indicators. These categories are not disclosed in the privacy policy’s employee data section. The consent records also show the in-platform prompt is branded as Meridian HR but does not identify the data controller. The notice does not clearly disclose the sensitive nature of the data, retention period, rights process, controller identity, or whether individual responses are shared. | High |
| 4 | Workforce benchmarking analytics are omitted or materially understated | Privacy Policy §§3, 4, 5, 6; ROPA PROC-007; consent records WC-0013 through WC-0014 | The ROPA shows Meridian uses derived employee data across clients for benchmarking, market research, product improvement, and add-on benchmarking reports sold to clients. Meridian acts as an independent controller for this activity and retains data indefinitely. The privacy policy does not clearly disclose this cross-client secondary use, the commercial benchmarking purpose, Meridian’s controller role, the indefinite retention period, or the use of Snowflake in the United States for this processing. | High |
| 5 | Analytics disclosures incorrectly state reports are aggregated and anonymized | Privacy Policy §3; ROPA PROC-006 and PROC-007 | The privacy policy states workforce analytics reports are shared with employers in “aggregated, anonymized form.” However, the ROPA states that for performance analytics reporting, the aggregation threshold is waived for executive-level employees and individual-level reports are provided for C-suite employees for 23 clients. This contradicts the privacy notice and may mislead data subjects about the identifiability of analytics outputs. | High |
| 6 | Employee personal data categories are incomplete | Privacy Policy §2; ROPA PROC-002, PROC-005, PROC-008 | The policy lists many employee data categories but omits or under-describes several material categories in the ROPA, including bank account details, tax codes, pension contributions, dependent information, disability status, and wellness/mental health indicators. Because the platform processes compensation, benefits, payroll, health-related, and wellness data, the notice should clearly disclose these categories. | Medium |
| 7 | Third-party processor and subprocessor disclosures are incomplete | Privacy Policy §5; ROPA PROC-007, PROC-009, PROC-010, PROC-011, PROC-012 | The privacy policy lists AWS, Stripe, SendGrid, Snowflake, and HubSpot, but the ROPA also identifies Google Analytics and Zendesk. The policy does not disclose Google Analytics for website tracking or Zendesk for customer support. In addition, the ROPA shows several vendors are in the United States, but the privacy policy does not clearly disclose international transfers or related safeguards. | Medium |
| 8 | Retention disclosures are inconsistent with actual processing records | Privacy Policy §6; ROPA PROC-003, PROC-005, PROC-007, PROC-008; DSAR log DSAR-2025-HR-009 and DSAR-2025-HR-013 | The policy says employee data is retained for the client contract term plus 90 days, with longer retention where legally required. The ROPA shows several materially different retention periods: seven years post-termination for employment records, client contract plus seven years for benefits, 12 months for wellness survey data, and indefinite retention for benchmarking analytics. The DSAR log also shows former employee requests rejected because data was “not available,” which conflicts with stated longer retention practices. The notice is therefore not sufficiently precise or consistent. | High |
| 9 | Data subject rights notice is incomplete for Meridian-controlled processing | Privacy Policy §7; ROPA PROC-007, PROC-009, PROC-010, PROC-011, PROC-012; DSAR log DSAR-2025-HR-011 | The privacy policy directs employees whose data is processed through the platform to contact their employer’s HR department. That may be appropriate for processor-only activities, but it is insufficient where Meridian acts as controller, including workforce benchmarking, website analytics, sales CRM, email marketing, and customer support. The DSAR log shows at least one employee request was rejected as a processor request without checking whether the data subject’s data was included in Meridian-controlled processing activities. | High |
| 10 | Consent notices do not identify the controller or provide sufficient privacy information | Consent records; Privacy Policy §§4, 7; ROPA PROC-005 and PROC-007 | The consent records show all wellness and performance analytics consent forms are branded as Meridian HR and do not identify the employer or Meridian as controller. The consent text is brief and does not provide sufficient detail on purposes, data categories, recipients, retention, withdrawal consequences, or rights. This creates ambiguity and weakens the validity of the notice associated with optional processing. | High |
| 11 | Legal basis disclosures are too generic and inconsistent with processing records | Privacy Policy §4; ROPA PROC-005, PROC-006, PROC-007; consent records | The policy lists broad legal bases but does not map them to specific processing activities. The ROPA identifies workforce benchmarking as legitimate interest, wellness survey as consent, performance analytics reporting as contractual necessity, and email marketing as consent. Consent records also reference performance analytics and benchmarking consent, creating inconsistency with the ROPA’s stated legal bases. Data subjects are not given clear notice of which legal basis applies to which purpose. | Medium |
| 12 | Notice of policy updates is limited to administrators, not affected data subjects | Privacy Policy §9 | The policy says material changes will be communicated to customer administrators at least 30 days before taking effect. There is no indication that employees, former employees, dependents, beneficiaries, or other affected data subjects will be notified directly or through employer-controlled communications. For a privacy notice control, Meridian should demonstrate how notice changes reach the relevant data subjects. | Medium |
